story.heading: The Story of Mongra
paragraphs: Deep in the Karewa plateau of Pampore — just south of Srinagar — the saffron crocus has bloomed every October for over 700 years. Pampore produces 90% of India's saffron, and its terroir is irreplaceable: clay-rich, well-drained soil, sharp autumn nights, and dawn mists from Dal Lake.

Mongra is the highest grade of Kashmiri saffron — only the deep-red stigma tips, separated by hand from the yellow style. Just 3 stigmas per flower; roughly 150,000 flowers for one kilogram. We have sourced Mongra grade exclusively since 2004, working directly with farming families who have tended these fields for generations.
story.imageAlt: Saffron crocus fields in Pampore, Kashmir at dawn

items.q: How do I know the saffron is genuine?
items.a: Every batch is lab-tested to ISO 3632 Grade A standards for crocin, picrocrocin, and safranal. A lab certificate is available on request — and you can verify it yourself with [three simple at-home tests](products.html#identify).
Keep the tin airtight, away from light and humidity. Stored correctly, Mongra saffron keeps its potency for 36 months.

items.q: How much saffron should I use in a dish?
items.a: 8–15 threads is enough for most recipes serving 2–4 people. Always bloom the threads in warm (not boiling) liquid for 10–20 minutes first — see [our recipes](recipes.html) for exact amounts.

identify.heading: How to Identify Real Saffron
identify.sub: 85% of saffron sold globally is adulterated. Here's how to know yours is genuine — in three simple tests.

steps.title: The Water Test
steps.text: Place a few threads in cold water. Real saffron slowly releases a deep golden-yellow colour over 10–15 minutes. It won't bleed instantly — and the threads remain crimson throughout.

steps.title: The Smell Test
steps.text: Rub a few threads between your fingers. Genuine Mongra saffron has a complex, honey-like aroma with hay and metallic notes — never artificial or chemical.

steps.title: The Shape Test
steps.text: Each thread should be trumpet-shaped — narrow at one end, widening to a feathered top. Mongra grade has no yellow style attached. Any yellow at the base is lower-grade Lachha or blended saffron.

recipesPage.sub: Six tested recipes with full ingredients and step-by-step methods — from Kashmiri classics to UAE favourites. Every recipe is print-friendly.

golden.heading: Always Bloom Your Saffron First
golden.sub: Whatever you're making, bloom your Mongra saffron threads in warm (not boiling) liquid for 10–20 minutes before adding to your dish. This releases the crocin pigment and safranal aroma, giving maximum colour, flavour, and benefit from every thread.

recipes.name: Kashmiri Kahwa
recipes.cardDesc: The jewel of Kashmiri hospitality — fragrant green tea scented with saffron, cardamom, and cinnamon.
recipes.schemaDesc: Traditional Kashmiri green tea with saffron, cardamom, and cinnamon.
recipes.imageAlt: Kashmiri Kahwa — green tea with saffron threads and almonds
ingredients: 1 tbsp green tea leaves (or 1 green tea bag)
steps: Bring water to a boil in a small saucepan. Add cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon. Simmer 5 minutes until fragrant.
Add the green tea leaves and saffron threads. Remove from heat and steep for 3 minutes — no longer, or the tea becomes bitter.
Strain into small cups. Add honey or sugar to taste.
Garnish with slivered almonds and a few drops of rose water. Serve immediately.
recipes.tip: **Saffron tip:** Bloom the threads in 1 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes before brewing for deeper colour. Never add saffron to boiling water — heat above 85°C degrades the aromatic safranal compound.

recipes.name: Zafrani Pulao
recipes.cardDesc: Fragrant saffron rice with caramelised onions and whole spices — the ceremonial rice of Kashmir.
recipes.schemaDesc: Fragrant Kashmiri saffron rice with caramelised onions and whole spices.
recipes.imageAlt: Zafrani Pulao — golden saffron rice with whole spices
ingredients: 2 cups aged basmati rice, soaked 30 minutes
1 bay leaf, 4 cloves, 4 cardamoms, 1 cinnamon stick
2 tbsp fried cashews and raisins to garnish
steps: Bloom saffron in warm milk for 20 minutes until the milk turns rich gold.
Heat ghee in a heavy pot. Fry whole spices 1 minute, then fry onions on medium until deep golden-brown, about 15 minutes.
Drain the rice and stir gently to coat in ghee. Add fennel powder, sonth, and salt.
Pour in 3½ cups water. Boil, then cover and simmer on low for 10 minutes.
Drizzle the saffron milk over the rice without stirring. Re-cover and steam on the lowest heat 12 more minutes.
Rest 5 minutes, fluff with a fork, and garnish with fried cashews and raisins.
recipes.tip: **Saffron tip:** Bloom in warm (not hot) milk — about 60°C, warm on the wrist but not scalding. This preserves the aromatics while extracting full colour.

recipes.name: Kesar Doodh
recipes.cardDesc: Warm saffron milk — the traditional wellness drink, served before sleep for over a thousand years.
recipes.schemaDesc: Warm saffron milk — the traditional Kashmiri wellness drink.
recipes.imageAlt: Kesar Doodh — warm golden saffron milk with pistachios
ingredients: Pistachios and a few saffron threads for garnish
steps: Bloom the saffron in 1 tbsp warm milk for 10 minutes until deep amber.
Warm the remaining milk until steaming — around 70°C, do not boil.
Add crushed cardamom and stir for 1 minute.
Pour into your cup, add the saffron bloom and honey, and stir gently.
Garnish with slivered pistachios and 2–3 saffron threads. Drink warm before bed.
recipes.tip: **Wellness note:** About 8–10 Mongra threads (~30mg) is the amount used in most published research on saffron's benefits — Kesar Doodh is the simplest daily way to enjoy it.

recipes.name: Arabic Saffron Machboos
recipes.cardDesc: The Gulf's celebrated rice dish — slow-cooked spiced chicken over fragrant rice, finished with saffron and rose water.
recipes.schemaDesc: Gulf-style spiced chicken and rice finished with saffron and rose water.
recipes.imageAlt: Arabic Saffron Machboos — spiced chicken over saffron rice
ingredients: 1 whole chicken, cut into pieces (or 1kg thighs)
2 cups long-grain or basmati rice, soaked
15 Mongra saffron threads + 4 tbsp warm water
3 onions, finely chopped · 4 cloves garlic, minced
1 tsp turmeric · 1 tsp cumin · ½ tsp black lime powder
Fresh coriander and fried onions to serve
steps: Bloom saffron in 4 tbsp warm water for 20 minutes. Set aside.
Fry onions in oil until golden-brown (15–20 minutes). Add garlic; fry 2 more minutes.
Brown the chicken pieces, about 8 minutes. Add baharat, turmeric, cumin, and loomi powder; stir to coat.
Add tomatoes and whole loomi. Cook 10 minutes, then add stock, boil, and simmer covered 30 minutes.
Remove chicken; strain the broth (you need 3 cups). Cook the rice in the broth, covered, 15 minutes.
Place chicken on the rice, drizzle with saffron water and rose water, and steam on very low heat 15 minutes.
Serve on a platter, garnished with coriander and crispy fried onions.
recipes.tip: **Saffron tip:** Add the saffron bloom and rose water together in the final steaming stage — this preserves the floral top notes that a long simmer would destroy.

recipes.name: Saffron Panna Cotta
recipes.cardDesc: A silky Italian custard elevated with Kashmiri saffron — ivory and gold in a glass.
recipes.schemaDesc: Silky Italian cream dessert infused with Kashmiri saffron.
recipes.imageAlt: Saffron Panna Cotta — golden cream dessert with pistachio
ingredients: To serve: honey, pistachio crumble, saffron threads
steps: Sprinkle gelatin over cold water; bloom 5 minutes.
Heat cream with saffron and sugar until steaming, stirring to dissolve the sugar. Do not boil.
Stir in vanilla, then whisk in the bloomed gelatin until fully dissolved.
Strain into glasses or ramekins. Cool, then refrigerate at least 4 hours.
Serve drizzled with honey, pistachio crumble, and 2–3 saffron threads on top.
recipes.tip: **Chef's note:** The saffron infuses as the cream heats — don't strain until step 4. That's what gives the golden hue and full flavour without graininess.

recipes.name: Saffron Lemonade
recipes.cardDesc: A refreshing UAE party staple — fresh lemon balanced by sweet, floral saffron syrup.
recipes.schemaDesc: Refreshing lemonade with sweet, floral Mongra saffron syrup.
recipes.imageAlt: Saffron lemonade — golden iced drink with lemon and mint
ingredients: 10 Mongra saffron threads + 3 tbsp warm water
½ cup (120ml) fresh lemon juice (4–5 lemons)
⅓ cup (80ml) simple syrup (1:1 sugar and water)
3 cups cold water or sparkling water
Lemon slices, mint, and saffron threads to garnish
steps: Bloom saffron in 3 tbsp warm (not hot) water for 10 minutes until amber-gold.
In a pitcher, combine lemon juice, simple syrup, and the saffron bloom. Stir well.
Add cold water (or sparkling for fizz). Taste and adjust sweetness.
Pour over ice and garnish with lemon, mint, and a few floating saffron threads.
recipes.tip: **UAE twist:** Swap the syrup for 3 tbsp of our [Saffron Honey](products.html) — extraordinary for Iftar and Eid gatherings.

posts.title: What is Mongra Grade Saffron and Why It Matters
posts.excerpt: Walk into any spice market from Dubai to Delhi and you'll find saffron priced from AED 10 to AED 300 per gram. The reason lies in a single word: grade.
posts.body:
Kashmiri saffron is classified into three grades based on which part of the crocus stigma is used. Mongra — the highest — consists entirely of the deep-red stigma tips, separated from the yellow style. Below that is Lachha, which includes the red part plus some yellow style, and Zarda or Gucchi, which includes the entire stigma with the full yellow portion. Each step down in grade means less crocin (the colourant), less picrocrocin (the bitter principle), and less safranal (the aroma compound).

Mongra saffron gives 20–30% more colour per gram than Lachha grade. In a Zafrani Pulao or a pot of Kahwa, the difference is immediately visible — a rich golden hue versus a pale yellow tint. In aroma, Mongra releases a deep, honeyed scent that lingers long after cooking; lower grades often smell grassy or of almost nothing at all.

The labour involved is extreme. Each saffron crocus produces just 3 stigmas. After hand-harvesting the flowers at dawn — the only time they're open — workers separate the three red filaments from the yellow style by hand, one flower at a time. One kilogram of Mongra requires roughly 150,000 flowers and over 100 hours of skilled hand work.

When you buy Mongra grade, you're buying the most concentrated, potent form of one of the world's most ancient spices. At Saffron of Kashmir, we source exclusively Mongra grade, tested and certified to ISO 3632 Grade A standards.

posts.title: The Saffron Fields of Pampore: A 700-Year Legacy
posts.excerpt: Twenty kilometres south of Srinagar lies Pampore — the "Saffron Town of India" — whose Karewa plateau produces 90% of India's saffron and some of the finest on earth.
posts.body:
Saffron cultivation in Kashmir stretches back to at least the 14th century, when Persian traders are believed to have introduced the crocus to the Valley. The Mughal emperors were among the most ardent patrons of Kashmiri saffron — Akbar reportedly ordered saffron planted in royal gardens across the subcontinent, but only the Pampore variety produced the quality he sought. The reason is the soil: the Karewa formation is a rare geological deposit of glacial silt, mineral-rich, with perfect drainage for the moisture-sensitive crocus.

The harvest happens in a dramatic 15-to-20-day window in October and November, when the purple flowers emerge almost overnight. Entire farming families move into the fields before dawn, picking flowers by hand before the sun opens them fully. Delay even an hour, and the petals wilt and the stigmas lose potency — one of the most time-pressured harvests in the world.

By the early 2000s, the industry was in crisis: declining groundwater, erratic snowfall, and competition from cheaper Iranian saffron pushed many farmers to abandon the crop. The area under cultivation fell from 5,700 hectares in the 1990s to under 2,500 by 2010.

Saffron of Kashmir was founded in 2004 precisely to address this — establishing direct partnerships with Pampore farming families at fair prices, giving them the economic incentive to keep growing what the Mughals prized above all spices. Every tin of our saffron represents a direct line from these ancient fields to your kitchen.

posts.title: How to Test Saffron Purity at Home (3 Methods)
posts.excerpt: A 2019 study found over 50% of commercially available saffron in the Middle East contained adulterants — dyed corn silk, safflower petals, even coloured paper fibres. Here's how to verify yours.
posts.body:
## Method 1: The Cold Water Soak

Place 3–4 threads in a glass of cold water and watch for 10–15 minutes. Real saffron slowly releases a deep golden-yellow colour while the thread itself stays crimson. Fake (dyed) saffron bleaches almost immediately, turning pale while the water goes a harsh orange-red.

## Method 2: The Baking Soda Test

Add a pinch of baking soda to your saffron water. Real saffron turns the water bright golden-yellow. Adulterated saffron often turns it red, green, or murky brown as the alkaline soda reacts with synthetic pigments.

## Method 3: The Rub Test

Rub a single thread between your fingers. Genuine saffron stains your fingers golden-yellow and softens without crumbling. Fake threads bleed colour quickly and disintegrate, often leaving an unnaturally bright orange-red dye.

*Note:* These are consumer tests. Professional testing uses HPLC spectrophotometry to measure crocin concentration and ISO 3632 compliance. All our saffron is batch-tested to Grade A standards — certificate available on request.

posts.title: Using Kashmiri Saffron in Arabic Cuisine
posts.excerpt: Saffron has been part of Arab cuisine for over a thousand years — from celebratory Machboos to saffron-scented Gahwa and the delicate Umm Ali dessert.
posts.body:
**Machboos (Kabsa):** The Gulf's answer to biryani. Use 8–10 threads of Mongra saffron, bloomed for 20 minutes in 3 tablespoons of warm (not boiling) water, then added with rose water in the final steaming stage. The goal is a subtle golden colour and floral perfume — not an aggressive yellow. [Full Machboos recipe here.](recipes.html)

**Saffron Gahwa:** Traditional Arabic cardamom coffee with a pinch of saffron added to the grounds. Use just 3–4 threads per pot — saffron is a supporting note here, adding warmth and a faint bittersweet edge.

**Saffron Milk (Haleeb Zafaran):** Bloom 8–10 threads in a tablespoon of warm milk, then add to a full warm cup with honey, cardamom, and pistachios — the Arabic equivalent of Kesar Doodh, drunk daily across Emirati households.

**The golden rule:** Always bloom saffron in warm liquid (water, milk, stock — never oil) before adding it to your dish, and never grind Mongra saffron dry — it destroys the delicate aromatic compounds.

posts.title: Health Benefits of Saffron Backed by Research
posts.excerpt: Saffron has been used medicinally for over 3,000 years in Persian, Ayurvedic, and Unani traditions. Modern research is catching up.
posts.body:
## Mood and mental health

A 2014 meta-analysis in *Human Psychopharmacology* reviewed five randomised controlled trials and found saffron supplementation (30mg/day) significantly more effective than placebo for mild-to-moderate depression. The compound responsible appears to be crocin, which influences serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine pathways.

## Antioxidant properties

Saffron contains more than 150 compounds, including crocin, crocetin, and safranal — among the most powerful plant antioxidants identified, able to neutralise reactive oxygen species and reduce lipid peroxidation.

## Vision and eye health

Studies from the University of L'Aquila (Italy) found daily saffron supplementation improved vision in patients with age-related macular degeneration. The crocetin in saffron appears to improve oxygen transport to the retina and protect photoreceptor cells.

## Dosage note

Research doses range from 20–100mg per day; a generous pinch of Mongra saffron (~30mg) in your daily Kahwa or Kesar Doodh falls within this range. This is not medical advice — consult your healthcare provider for therapeutic applications.
